coredata.dc:title: Microstructure and hydrogen permeability in Nb–Ti–Co multiphase alloys
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2006 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Hashi, K.
Ishikawa, K.
Matsuda, T.
Aoki, K.
coredata.dc:description: Microstructure and hydrogen permeability (Φ) of as-cast Nb–Ti–Co alloys have been investigated to find out alloys combining high hydrogen permeability and large resistance to the hydrogen embrittlement by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and by the gas permeation technique, respectively. Hydrogen permeable alloys are obtained near the line connecting the TiCo compound and pure Nb, and about 50mol% Nb and less. These alloys are classified into three groups, i.e. (1) the primary TiCo phase and the eutectic {TiCo+(Nb, Ti)} phase, (2) TiCo+(Nb, Ti)+the eutectic {TiCo+(Nb, Ti)} phase and (3) the primary (Nb, Ti)+the eutectic {TiCo+(Nb, Ti)} phase. The present work indicates that the eutectic phase suppresses the hydrogen embrittlement of the Nb–Ti–Co alloys. The eutectic Nb30Ti35Co35 alloy exhibits large resistance to the hydrogen embrittlement and the highest Φ of 2.64×10−8 molH2 m−1 s−1 Pa−0.5 at 673K, which is 1.7 times higher than that of pure Pd.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrogen permeation
Hydrogen embrittlement
TiCo compound
Eutectic structure

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The microstructure that suppresses the hydrogen embrittlement in the Nb–Ti–Co alloys

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Chemical compositions (mol%) of the alloys investigated in the present work. The solid squares and circles denote the brittle alloys in the as-cast state and the broken ones by hydrogenation, respectively. The open triangles and circles denote the alloys for which the hydrogen permeation experiments are possible.
An SEM photograph of the Nb21Ti20Co59 (A) alloy that is brittle in the as-cast state.
An SEM photograph of the Nb10Ti60Co30 (B) alloy consisting of the primary TiCo phase+the eutectic {(Nb, Ti)+Ti2Co} phase. The value of Φ for this alloy is less than 1×10−9 molH2 m−1 s−1 Pa−0.5.
SEM photographs of (a) the Nb29Ti51Co20 (C) and (b) Nb49Ti31Co20 (D) alloys broken down during the hydrogen permeation experiments.
The plot of Φ in the Nb x Ti60−x Co40 alloys as well as pure Pd in the form of the Arrhenius plot.
SEM photographs of the hydrogen permeable (a) Nb20Ti39Co41 (E) and (b) Nb30Ti30Co40 (F) alloys.
The plot of the Φ values for the Nb x Ti70−x Co30 alloys as well as Pd in the form of the Arrhenius plot.
SEM photographs of the hydrogen permeable Nb19Ti50Co31 (G) and the Nb29T40Co31 (H) alloys.
SEM photographs of the hydrogen permeable Nb38Ti31Co31 (I) and the Nb50Ti20Co30 (J) alloys.
The plot of the Φ values in the Nb30Ti70−x Co x alloys as well as Pd in the form of the Arrhenius plot.
An SEM photograph of the hydrogen permeable Nb30Ti35Co35 (K) alloy. This alloy consists of only the eutectic {(Nb, Ti)+TiCo} phase with fine lamellar microstructures.
The Φ values of the Nb–Ti–Co alloys at 673K. The figures under the open circles indicate the hydrogen permeability Φ (10−8 molH2 m−1 s−1 Pa−0.5).
Chemical composition (mol%) of the (A)–(K) alloys along with their constituting phases and their compositions (mol%) in the Nb–Ti–Co system

row.entry: Chemical composition of the constituting phases (mol%)

article.ce:title: Microstructure and hydrogen permeability in Nb–Ti–Co multiphase alloys

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science, Kitami Institute of Technology, Koen-cho 165, Kitami, Hokkaido 090-8507, Japan

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Present address: The Japan Steel Works, Ltd., Chatsumachi 4, Muroran, Hokkaido 051-8505, Japan.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Microstructure and hydrogen permeability (Φ) of as-cast Nb–Ti–Co alloys have been investigated to find out alloys combining high hydrogen permeability and large resistance to the hydrogen embrittlement by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and by the gas permeation technique, respectively. Hydrogen permeable alloys are obtained near the line connecting the TiCo compound and pure Nb, and about 50mol% Nb and less. These alloys are classified into three groups, i.e. (1) the primary TiCo phase and the eutectic {TiCo+(Nb, Ti)} phase, (2) TiCo+(Nb, Ti)+the eutectic {TiCo+(Nb, Ti)} phase and (3) the primary (Nb, Ti)+the eutectic {TiCo+(Nb, Ti)} phase. The present work indicates that the eutectic phase suppresses the hydrogen embrittlement of the Nb–Ti–Co alloys. The eutectic Nb30Ti35Co35 alloy exhibits large resistance to the hydrogen embrittlement and the highest Φ of 2.64×10−8 molH2 m−1 s−1 Pa−0.5 at 673K, which is 1.7 times higher than that of pure Pd.

ce:section.ce:para:
Hydrogen gas is not produced as a natural resource. Then, it must be produced by electrolysis of water or by steam reforming of natural gas (CH4) and so on. Hydrogen gas produced by steam reforming contains much CO gas that damages the Pt electrode of fuel cells, so that such hydrogen gas must be purified using some techniques [1]. Pd and Pd–Ag alloys are usually used as hydrogen permeation alloys for separation and purification of hydrogen gas [2], but they are too expensive and scarce resources. Furthermore, the price of Pd changes suddenly and frequently. Then, the development of low cost and high performance hydrogen permeation alloys other than Pd alloys is strongly desired [3]. Hydrogen permeation flux, J, through the hydrogen permeation alloy membrane is described on the basis of the Fick's first law and the Sieverts’ law as follows:
(1)
J =
D K
Δ
P
1 / 2
L
=
Φ
Δ
P
1 / 2
L
Here, D and K are the hydrogen diffusion coefficient and the hydrogen solubility coefficient, respectively, and the product of them is hydrogen permeability Φ. ΔP 1/2 and L are the difference of square root of the hydrogen pressure at both sides of the membrane and the thickness of the membrane, respectively. The first way to attain large J is to find out alloys having the high Φ value. It is well known that Φ values of 5A metals such as V, Nb and Ta are higher than that of Pd and Pd–Ag alloys [4], however these metals and their alloys suffer from the severe hydrogen embrittlement [5–9]. The second way to attain large J is to make a pressure difference large and to reduce the thickness of the membrane. For such purposes, the high yield strength and large resistance to the hydrogen embrittlement are essential. However, the suppression of the hydrogen embrittlement is generally very difficult in single-phase alloys, because high Φ requires large K, which gives to the hydrogen embrittlement. Recently, the present authors have proposed new hydrogen permeation alloys on the basis of new concept of duplex phases [10,11]. They have found out that the Nb–Ti–Ni alloys containing much eutectic phase that consists of the B2–TiNi compound and the bcc-(Nb, Ti) solid solution have large resistance to the hydrogen embrittlement. Furthermore, they have shown that Φ increases with increasing the volume fraction of the primary bcc-(Nb, Ti) phase [12]. The alloys consisting of a combination of the eutectic phase with the primary one show high hydrogen permeability and the large resistance to hydrogen embrittlement [10–12]. It is well known that TiCo is also a B2-type intermetallic compound. If the eutectic phase is formed in the Nb–Ti–Co system, there is a possibility that the Nb–Ti–Co alloys permeate much hydrogen without the hydrogen embrittlement. The purpose of the present work is to find out alloys combining high hydrogen permeability and large resistance to the hydrogen embrittlement. In the present work, microstructure and hydrogen permeability Φ of as-cast Nb–Ti–Co alloys are systematically investigated.

Nb x Ti y Co(100−x–y) alloys (mol%) were prepared by arc melting using Nb (99.9mass% purity), Ti (99.5mass% purity) and Co (99.9mass% purity) in an argon atmosphere. Disk samples of 12mm in diameter and 0.7mm in thickness were cut using a spark erosion wire-cutting machine. Microstructural examinations were carried out with a scanning electron microscope (SEM). Determination of volume percentage of a phase was carried out on a Macintosh 〈model〉 computer using the public domain NIH image program. Chemical compositions were determined using an energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS) attached to SEM. The disk samples were polished with an abrasive paper, buff and α-alumina of 0.5μm particle size, followed by coating with Pd of 190nm in thickness using an RF magnetron-sputtering machine to avoid oxidation and to enhance the dissociation of hydrogen molecules to atoms. The disk sample sealed with copper gaskets was set into the hydrogen permeation measuring apparatus, which has been described in the previous paper [10]. Both sides of the disk were first evacuated using a diffusion pump up to below 3×10−3 Pa, and then the sample was heated to 673K and kept at this temperature. High purity hydrogen (99.99999% purity) of 0.1 and 0.2MPa was introduced into the downstream and the upstream sides, respectively, followed by hydrogen permeation measurement. The hydrogen pressure on the upstream side was raised from 0.2 to 0.97MPa and the temperature was stepped down from 673 to 523K at 50K intervals. Hydrogen flux J (molH2 m−2 s−1) was measured using a mass flow meter. Hydrogen permeability, Φ, was determined from the slope of the relation between (J × L) and ΔP 1/2.

Fig. 1 shows chemical compositions (mol%) of the Nb–Ti–Co alloys investigated in the present work. Chemical compositions (mol%) of the representative alloys (A)–(K) and their constituting phases are tabulated in Table 1 . Solid squares and solid circles denote brittle alloys in the as-cast state and fractured ones during the hydrogen permeation measurement, respectively. On the other hand, open circles denote alloys for which the hydrogen permeation measurement is possible and their values of hydrogen permeability Φ are of the order of 10−9 and 10−8 molH2 m−1 s−1 Pa−0.5. Open triangle denotes alloys for which values of Φ are of the order of 10−9 molH2 m−1 s−1 Pa−0.5 or less.

Fig. 2 shows an SEM photograph of the Nb21Ti20Co59 (A) alloy that is broken down during sealing by the copper gaskets. This alloy consists of the primary Co2Nb (white phase) and the eutectic {TiCo+Co2Nb} phases. Since the C14 Laves phase Co2Nb is brittle, the Nb21Ti20Co59 (A) alloy containing much amount of Co2Nb is broken down. Similarly, alloys denoted by the solid squares are brittle, because they contain much amount of brittle phases such as TiCo2, Co6Nb7 and so on.

Fig. 3 shows an SEM photograph of the Nb10Ti60Co30 (B) alloy consisting of the primary phase TiCo and the eutectic {(Nb, Ti)+Ti2Co} phase. This alloy can permeate a little amount of hydrogen, and its Φ value is of the order of 10−10 molH2 m−1 s−1 Pa−0.5 at 673K.

Fig. 4 shows SEM photographs of (a) the Nb29Ti51Co20 (C) and (b) Nb49Ti31Co20 (D) alloys that are broken down during the hydrogen permeation measurements. White, black and gray phases are the (Nb, Ti) solid solution, the TiCo compound and the eutectic {(Nb, Ti)+TiCo} phase, respectively. The (Nb, Ti) phase is solidified as the primary phase in these alloys. The reason why these alloys are broken down by the hydrogen embrittlement is discussed latter.

Fig. 5 shows the plot of Φ in the Nb x Ti60−x Co40 alloys in the form of the Arrhenius plot. For reference, the Φ values of Pd determined using the apparatus and the procedure adopted in the present work are also plotted by the broken line, because their values vary widely depending on the researches. These values of Φ for Pd are also plotted in Figs. 7 and 10. The Φ value of Pd is 1.56×10−8 molH2 m−1 s−1 Pa−0.5 at 673K. The Φ value in the Nb10Ti60Co30 alloy is 0.49×10−8 molH2 m−1 s−1 Pa−0.5 at 673K, but is unmeasurable below 623K due to the hydrogen embrittlement. On the other hand, the Φ values for the Nb20Ti39Co41 (E) and the Nb30Ti30Co40 (F) alloys increase with increasing temperature, but depend slightly on the Nb content. The Φ value of the Nb20Ti39Co41 (E) alloy is 1.26×10−8 molH2 m−1 s−1 Pa−0.5 at 673K, which is slightly lower than that of pure Pd.

Fig. 6 shows SEM photographs of the hydrogen permeable (a) Nb20Ti39Co41 (E) and (b) Nb30Ti30Co40 (F) alloys. Black and bright gray phases are the TiCo compound and the eutectic {(Nb, Ti)+TiCo} phase, respectively. The TiCo compound is solidified as the primary phase in these alloys. It is worth notice that the Nb20Ti39Co41 (E) alloy consisting of a combination of the primary TiCo phase with the eutectic {(Nb, Ti)+TiCo} phase shows a high Φ value that is comparable to that of pure Pd at 673K.

Fig. 7 shows the plot of the Φ values for the Nb x Ti70−x Co30 alloys as well as Pd in the form of the Arrhenius plot. The Φ values for these alloys increase with increasing temperature, although the slopes of these lines, i.e. the activation energies for hydrogen permeation, depend strongly on the Nb content. The Φ value for the Nb19Ti50Co31 (G) alloy shows the largest temperature dependence, but is unmeasurable below 573K, which implies that this alloy is not so strong against the hydrogen embrittlement. The Φ value for the Nb29T40Co31 (H) alloy shows the secondary large temperature dependence and its Φ value is 2.50×10−8 molH2 m−1 s−1 Pa−0.5 at 673K, which is highest in these alloys. The temperature dependences of Φ for the Nb38Ti31Co31 (I) and the Nb50Ti20Co30 (J) alloys are nearly same as that of the Nb20Ti39Co41 (E) alloy. It is worth notice that the Φ values for the alloys excepting for the Nb50Ti20Co30 (J) alloy are higher than that of pure Pd at 673K.

Fig. 8 shows SEM photographs of (a) the hydrogen permeable Nb19Ti50Co31 (G) and (b) the hydrogen permeable Nb29T40Co31 (H) alloys. White, black and mottled phases are the (Nb, Ti) solid solution, the TiCo compound, and the eutectic {TiCo+(Nb, Ti)} phase, respectively. The TiCo compound and the (Nb, Ti) solid solution are main phases in the Nb19Ti50Co31 (G) alloy, while the eutectic {(Nb, Ti)+TiCo} phase is a minor one. On the other hand, these three phases are main ones in the Nb29T40Co31 (H) alloy.

Fig. 9 shows SEM photographs of (a) the hydrogen permeable Nb38Ti31Co31 (I) and (b) the Nb50Ti20Co30 (J) alloys. White, black and gray phases are the (Nb, Ti) solid solution, the TiCo compound and the eutectic {(Nb, Ti)+TiCo} phase, respectively. The (Nb, Ti) solid solution and the eutectic {(Nb, Ti)+TiCo} phase are main phases, while the TiCo compound is a minor one. The (Nb, Ti) solid solution is solidified as the primary phase in these alloys.

Fig. 10 shows the plot of the Φ values in the Nb30Ti70−x Co x alloys as well as Pd in the form of the Arrhenius plot. The Φ values for these alloys increase with increasing temperature. The Φ values for the Nb29T40Co31 (H) and the (K) Nb30Ti35Co35 are higher than that of Pd above 623K.

Fig. 11 shows an SEM photograph of the hydrogen permeable Nb30Ti35Co35 (K) alloy. This alloy consists of only the eutectic {(Nb, Ti)+TiCo} phase with fine lamellar microstructures. In order to check the extent of resistance to the hydrogen embrittlement, the Pd coated disk samples with the 0.4mm in thickness of the Nb30Ti35Co35 (K) and the Nb29T40Co31 (H) alloys are examined as follows. They are heated in vacuum at 673K for 2.5ks. Subsequently, (i) they are subjected to the hydrogen permeation measurement at the hydrogen pressure of 0.1MPa (at the downstream side) and at 0.5MPa H2 (at the upstream side) for 2.5ks, (ii) they are cooled down in a hydrogen atmosphere of 0.1MPa H2 (at the downstream side) and 0.2MPa H2 (at the upstream side) to room temperature and kept for 1.8ks and (iii) they are heated up to 673K in a hydrogen atmosphere of 0.1MPa H2 (at the downstream side) and 0.2MPa H2 (at the upstream side) and subsequently continues to the (i) step. The Nb29T40Co31 (H) alloy is broken down during the first cooling to room temperature. On the contrary, the Nb30Ti35Co35 (K) alloy does not break down even after three cycles of these procedures. Consequently, we can conclude that the eutectic Nb30Ti35Co35 (K) alloy is very strong against the hydrogen embrittlement.

Fig. 12 show the Φ values of the Nb–Ti–Co alloys at 673K. The figures under the open circles indicate the hydrogen permeability Φ (10−8 molH2 m−1 s−1 Pa−0.5). The Φ value for the eutectic Nb30Ti35Co35 (K) alloy is highest in these alloys and is 2.64×10−8 molH2 m−1 s−1 Pa−0.5 at 673K, which is 1.7 times larger than that of pure Pd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The microstructure that suppresses the hydrogen embrittlement in the Nb–Ti–Co alloys
ce:section.ce:para: The present work has demonstrated that the Nb–Ti–Co alloys are divided into two groups, i.e. the hydrogen permeable (open circle and open triangle) alloys and the hydrogen unpermeable (solid square and solid triangle) ones as shown in Fig. 1. The hydrogen permeation measurements are impossible in the alloys (solid square) containing much amount of the brittle compounds such as Co2Nb and Ti2Co. On the other hand, the Nb x Ti50−x Co50 and Nb10Ti60Co30 alloys (open triangle) show the low Φ value of the order of 10−9 molH2 m−1 s−1 Pa−0.5 or less. These alloys absorb a little amount of hydrogen, so that their Φ values are low and the hydrogen embrittlement does not occur. Fig. 1 indicates that the Nb x T30−x Co30 alloys can permeate hydrogen, but the Nb x T20−x Co20 ones are broken down by the hydrogen embrittlement. We discuss what factors suppress the hydrogen embrittlement of Nb–Ti–Co alloys. The Nb30Ti35Co35 (K) alloy consists of only eutectic {(Nb, Ti)+TiCo} phase with fine lamellar microstructure. This alloy is very strong against the hydrogen embrittlement, because it does not break down by cooling in a hydrogen atmosphere to room temperature. Therefore, it is suggested that the eutectic phase suppresses the hydrogen embrittlement in the Nb–Ti–Co alloys. Although the Nb49Ti31Co20 (D) and the Nb38Ti31Co31 (I) alloys consist of same kinds of phases, the former alloy is broken down when it is exposed to the hydrogen atmosphere of 0.2MPa at 673K, but the latter one is not broken down. Then, we compare the microstructures of them in order to know the factors that suppress the hydrogen embrittlement in the Nb–Ti–Co alloys. The volume fraction of the primary (Nb, Ti) phase, the TiCo compound and the eutectic {(Nb, Ti)+TiCo} phase in the Nb49Ti31Co20 (D) alloy is about 57, 18 and 25%, respectively. On the other hand, that of them in the Nb38Ti31Co31 (I) alloy is about 22.8 and 70%, respectively. That is, the volume fraction of the eutectic {(Nb, Ti)+TiCo} phase in the Nb38Ti31Co31 (I) alloy is 2.8 times larger than that in the Nb49Ti31Co20 (D) alloy. Similarly, the volume fractions of the eutectic phase are low in the NbxTi80−x Co20 alloys. On the contrary, the hydrogen permeable alloys such as the Nb30Ti30Co40 (F) and the Nb50Ti20Co30 (J) alloys contain much amount of the eutectic {(Nb, Ti)+TiCo} phase. Consequently, we conclude that the eutectic phase is effective to suppress the hydrogen embrittlement in the Nb–Ti–Co alloys. Ogawa et al. first reported that unidirectionally solidified LaNi5–Ni eutectic alloys possess better resistance to disintegration than the arc melted ones [13], although they did not mention whether eutectic phases in the as-cast alloy suppresses the hydrogen embrittlement or not. On the other hand, the present work supports strongly our previous reports that the hydrogen embrittlement is suppressed by the eutectic phase [10–12]. The elucidation of the mechanism for the suppression of the hydrogen embrittlement due to eutectic microstructure is the future important subject to be solved.

The present work has demonstrated that the eutectic Nb30Ti35Co35 (K) alloy has large resistance to the hydrogen embrittlement and shows the highest Φ of 2.64×10−8 molH2 m−1 s−1 Pa−0.5 at 673K. On the contrary, it has been reported that hydrogen permeability Φ in the eutectic Nb20Ti40Ni40 alloy is 1.11×10−8 molH2 m−1 s−1 Pa−0.5 at 673K and increases with increasing the volume fraction of the primary (Nb, Ti) phase in the Nb–Ti–Ni alloys [12]. Since the Φ value for pure Nb is highest among the metals, it is reasonable to note that Φ increases with increasing the volume fraction of the primary (Nb, Ti) as observed in the Nb–Ti–Ni alloys. It is strange that the Φ values of Nb-rich Nb–Ti–Co alloys, i.e. the Nb38Ti31Co31 (I) and the Nb50Ti20Co30 (J) alloys, containing much primary (Nb, Ti) phase are lower than that of the eutectic Nb30Ti35Co35 (K) alloy.

We discuss why the eutectic Nb30Ti35Co35 (K) alloy shows the highest value of Φ in the Nb–Ti–Co alloys. Several researchers have found an enhancement of the solubility and diffusivity of hydrogen in grain boundaries [14–16]. For instance, Arantes et al. reported that hydrogen permeation in nanocrystalline nickel is remarkably larger than that in microcrystalline one [14], which is due to increase of both hydrogen solubility and hydrogen diffusivity in grain boundaries. On the other hand, it is uncertain whether phase boundaries enhance hydrogen permeation or not, because the measurement of hydrogen diffusivity and solubility in phase boundaries is very difficult. Recently, Yamanaka et al. have investigated hydrogen permeation through Pd/Fe and Pd/Ni multiphase systems and reported that phase boundaries have no effect on the permeation [16]. However, they measured hydrogen permeation normal to the phase boundaries and their numbers are very limited. Then, there is a possibility that hydrogen permeation along the phase boundaries is enhanced by analogy with grain boundaries. The eutectic Nb30Ti35Co35 (K) alloy consists of a fine lamellar microstructure and contains much phase boundaries and grain boundaries. The high Φ of the eutectic phase is explainable on the basis of the enhancement of hydrogen permeation in the phase boundaries between TiNi and (Nb, Ti) and in grain boundaries.

Microstructure and hydrogen permeability (Φ) of the as-cast Nb–Ti–Co alloys have been investigated by SEM and by the gas permeation technique, respectively. The Φ value increases with increasing temperature for every alloy. The Nb30Ti35Co35 alloy consisting of the eutectic phase {(Nb, Ti)+TiCo} shows the highest Φ =2.64×10−8 molH2 m−1 s−1 Pa−0.5 at 673K, which is higher than that of pure Pd. The eutectic microstructure suppresses the hydrogen embrittlement of the Nb–Ti–Co alloys in the same way as Nb–Ti–Ni alloys. The present work demonstrates that duplex alloys containing eutectic microstructures are promising for new hydrogen permeation alloy membranes with large resistance to the hydrogen embrittlement.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work has been supported in part by a grant-in-aid for Scientific Research (B) of Japan Society for the Promotion of Science (JSPS).
